network.notes: Braga is the current Arkiv testnet. Always configure clients with the braga chain export.

privacy.explorerVisibility: Arkiv records (entityType, attributes, and payload) are visible on the Arkiv explorer and to network indexers by default. App-level ownership and createdBy/ownedBy filters control who your UI shows data to, but they do NOT hide the data from explorers or anyone reading the network directly.
privacy.rule: Whenever the user asks about private, confidential, secret, hidden, or restricted data — or about visibility, leaks, encryption, sensitive fields, or 'who can see this' — you MUST explicitly state that data written to Arkiv is visible on the explorer unless encrypted client-side before write. Never imply that ownership scoping alone makes data private from infrastructure.
guidance: Distinguish access privacy (what your app shows) from storage secrecy (what is visible on the network/explorer).
For true confidentiality, encrypt sensitive fields client-side before calling jsonToPayload, and manage decryption keys off-network.
Keep only non-sensitive indexing fields in attributes; put encrypted blobs in payload.
If a public summary is needed, write a separate sanitized public entity rather than relying on access filters to hide a private one.

bestPractices.title: Unique PROJECT_ATTRIBUTE per app
bestPractices.rule: Every app should define a unique PROJECT_ATTRIBUTE so records can be scoped to the product or deployment.
bestPractices.rationale: Lets queries isolate records for one app and avoids cross-app pollution on shared infrastructure.

bestPractices.title: Use entityType as the discriminator
bestPractices.rule: Prefer entityType as the app-level discriminator and include it as an indexed attribute alongside the project attribute.
bestPractices.rationale: Allows queries to fetch a single entity kind cleanly without overloading payload contents.

bestPractices.title: Attributes are query indexes; payload is the body
bestPractices.rule: Keep searchable, sortable, range-query, ownership, trust-boundary, and relationship fields in attributes. Store the richer record body in payload.
bestPractices.rationale: Attributes drive indexed lookups; payload carries the rest without bloating the query path.

bestPractices.title: Numeric attributes for sort and range queries
bestPractices.rule: Use numeric attributes for values that need ordering or range queries: createdAt, updatedAt, counters, scores, prices, expiration windows.
bestPractices.rationale: Numeric indexes support range scans; string timestamps do not sort reliably.

bestPractices.title: Use ExpirationTime helpers
bestPractices.rule: Use ExpirationTime helpers rather than hand-rolled expiration math. Choose durations intentionally for durable state vs short-lived drafts, jobs, or notifications.
bestPractices.rationale: Avoids drift between client and on-chain expiration logic.

bestPractices.title: Separate public read clients from wallet write clients
bestPractices.rule: Use public clients for browsing and querying. Use wallet clients only for signatures, writes, or batch mutations.
bestPractices.rationale: Avoids prompting wallet signatures on read paths and makes the trust boundary explicit.

bestPractices.title: Model unbounded relationships as separate entities
bestPractices.rule: Reactions, replies, follows, memberships, votes, events, logs, and ledgers should be their own entity with parent references as indexed attributes.
bestPractices.rationale: Embedded arrays grow unbounded and hurt query and write performance.

bestPractices.title: createdBy / ownedBy filters at trust boundaries
bestPractices.rule: Use createdBy or ownedBy filters when trust boundaries matter. Treat app/project, entity type, parent reference, and creator/owner filters as default query guardrails.
bestPractices.rationale: Prevents accidentally rendering records that don't belong to the asking user or app.

bestPractices.title: Defensively validate entity.toJson() payloads
bestPractices.rule: Parse, narrow, and handle malformed shapes gracefully before rendering or trusting payload data.
bestPractices.rationale: Payloads are user-written record bodies and can be malformed or adversarial.

bestPractices.title: Use mutateEntities for batch creates
bestPractices.rule: Use mutateEntities when multiple related entities should be written together or when ingestion jobs need throughput.
bestPractices.rationale: Avoids N round-trips for related records and keeps writes atomic at the batch level.

examplePatterns.title: Blog Challenge
patterns: Defines a product-scoped project attribute so queries isolate records for one app.
Uses entityType as a discriminator across blog-domain records.
Stores createdAt and updatedAt as numeric indexed attributes for sorting and timeline queries.
Uses .createdBy(...) query filtering for known author or creator boundaries.
Parses and validates entity.toJson() payloads before rendering.
Models reactions as separate entities instead of embedding an unbounded reaction list.

examplePatterns.title: Online Forum
patterns: Reply stores replyTo = postId so replies can be queried by parent post.
Models replies separately to keep thread growth unbounded.

examplePatterns.title: Dashboard Demo
patterns: Backend ingestion into Arkiv rather than only browser-originated writes.
Numeric timestamp attributes for sorting and incremental sync windows.
Paginated reads with hasNextPage() and next() for large result sets.

examplePatterns.title: SDK Sample
patterns: Separates public client reads from wallet client writes.
Uses jsonToPayload to convert structured JSON into an Arkiv payload.
Uses the query builder for attribute filters, creator/owner scoping, pagination, and typed access.

arkivFitVsAlternatives.arkivAdvantage: User-owned, wallet-authored, composable data without a product-owned database as the source of truth.
Queryable attributes and app-friendly mutation/query patterns rather than only content-addressed blobs or permanent files.
Stores app records directly and queries indexed attributes without depending on contract event history as the only source.

mustMention: attributes as query indexes and payload as record body
createdBy or ownedBy filters when trust boundaries matter
public read client vs wallet write client separation
mutateEntities for batch creates where applicable
